claims.text: The supplied materials include excerpts from govinfo_usc_17_107.pdf concerning Title 17 copyright fair-use materials and historical/revision notes.
claims.reason: One or more citation quotes are not verbatim substrings of cited evidence.

claims.text: The supplied materials include excerpts from govinfo_usc_17_105.pdf concerning Title 17 section 105 and related historical discussion.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The supplied materials include a newspaper-style excerpt from loc_evening_star_1900_01_01_0394.pdf discussing Potomac River improvement and estimated costs.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The Title 17 section 107 excerpt states that an unpublished work is not barred from a fair-use finding solely because it is unpublished, if the finding is made after consideration of the listed factors.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 historical notes describe fair use as a judicial doctrine and as a limitation on the exclusive rights of copyright owners.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 historical notes state that section 107 would give express statutory recognition to the fair-use doctrine for the first time.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 historical notes state that fair-use defenses had been raised in many copyright actions and that case law recognized and applied the doctrine.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 excerpt gives examples of activities courts might regard as fair use, including quotation in reviews or criticism, scholarly quotation, parody, news-report summaries, library reproduction to replace damaged copies, classroom illustration, legislative or judicial use, and incidental reproduction in a newsreel or broadcast.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that Judiciary Subcommittee members urged parties to meet independently regarding permissible educational uses of copyrighted material, and that the response was positive.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that meetings were held beginning in September 1975 among three groups addressing classroom reproduction of printed material, music, and audio-visual material.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that archival duplicate copies made to preserve deteriorating film materials fall within fair use.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that where copyrighted material contains unfair, inaccurate, or derogatory information about an individual or institution, that individual or institution may copy and reproduce necessary parts for understandable comment on the statements.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that publication of copyrighted material in Congressional hearings and documents would constitute fair use when the excerpt length, authorized copy count, and relevance to legitimate legislative concern are reasonable under the circumstances.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that whether copying portions of a newsletter is infringement or fair use depends on the facts of the individual case.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state as a general principle that the fair-use scope should be narrower for newsletters than for mass-circulation periodicals or scientific journals.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials identify the commercial nature of the user as a significant factor for newsletter copying and state that copying by a profit-making user of even a small portion may significantly affect the commercial market.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 107 materials state that a single-copy reproduction by a calligrapher of an excerpt from a copyrighted work for a single client does not represent copyright infringement.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 105 excerpt states that section 105 does not relieve the Government of its obligation to secure permission to publish a copyrighted work.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 105 excerpt states that Government publication or other use of a private work would not affect the work's copyright protection.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 105 excerpt states that section 105 is intended to restrict the prohibition against Government copyright to works written by U.S. Government employees within the scope of their official duties.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 105 excerpt describes a bill approach under which the Government agency concerned could decide whether an independent contractor or grantee may secure copyright in works prepared with Government funds.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The section 105 excerpt says the bill deliberately avoids an outright, unqualified prohibition against copyright in works prepared with Government funds.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.

claims.text: The newspaper excerpt states that the estimated cost for bridges, drainage, driveway, and embankment was placed at $135,000.
claims.reason: All citation quotes are grounded in cited evidence.
